# Transcrição: 2-LinkedIn Objectives

- **Fonte:** 2-LinkedIn Objectives
- **Duração:** 17:03
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

In this lesson, we're going to talk about your objectives on LinkedIn, where you're trying to push your audience, what you're trying to get them to do. And all this starts with a lot of people will come to us and they'll say, hey, LinkedIn ads, I've heard those are expensive, or I've done a little bit on there and the costs were just too high for me. And what I've seen a lot is most people will take what might be working already on Facebook and just trying to take that exact type of campaign or the exact type of ad, plug it into LinkedIn and hope and it's going to work. Now I'm not going to lie to you, the average click or cost per click on LinkedIn is typically on average going to be more than on Facebook. But just because that is doesn't mean you want to just go out there and spend your money without any sort of different strategy. So the biggest thing I want to get across in this lesson today is that LinkedIn ads are not Facebook ads. And you need to approach them with a different strategy in mind. So that's what we're going to start with today. Typically what this means for us is our clients that are using LinkedIn ads most successfully are selling something that's higher ticket. They're not selling $197 product. There's something that has some recurring revenue and the lifetime value of a client's going to be more. So the biggest thing, higher leverage opportunities on LinkedIn. How do you get someone quickly from an opt-in, from a lead magnet into a conversation, into a higher leverage sales opportunity? And for most of our clients appointments are that golden ticket. That is the area we're focusing is how quickly can I take someone, a prospect and get them into a phone call with you or your sales team. And that's what we're going to focus today. There's three top ways we're going to talk about in this training on how to do this. The first is referred, we refer to as RTA report to appointment. I'll go in more depth here on what that means. The second is our DTA direct to appointment. And then third is more of a retargeting play with what's called sponsored in mail. So we'll look at all three of these in this video today. So the RTA funnel, let's start here. The type of ad this is on LinkedIn is referred to as sponsored content. It's similar to your newsfeed ad on Facebook. It's going to show up when someone scroll on their homepage on LinkedIn. And here's what this looks like. So we're going to see like any sort of marketing campaign you have, it's going to start with defining the campaign avatar, specifically with LinkedIn, because I can get really granular on the types of industries I want to target and people with the direct job title that they have on LinkedIn. I like to make my different campaigns be really targeted on individual niches. So if I'm going to have, if I'm targeting both financial advisors and real estate agents, I don't want to dump those all into one. I want to split those up. And we'll talk more about that as we go through the training. Once you get your campaign campaign avatar decided on, you're going to create a sponsored post. We'll look at some of the creative and imagery you're going to use for that. From there, you would have this person fill out a lead form.

## [03:00 - 06:00]

And then you want to immediately after that, give them an opportunity to kind of cut to the front of the line. They raise their hands, they're interested in downloading a report or a web or attending a webinar or sign up for a video series, whatever lead magnet this might be. You want to give them an opportunity right after that to get more information or talk with someone on your team. So that's where we offer them a phone call on our thank you page. And then finally, we're going to deliver the report and follow up to try and book that call because again, we're looking to get these people, so we're paying a premium for this audience. We want you as soon as possible, get them into a higher leverage opportunity. Now I mentioned this as I was going through. We call this the RTA funnel, but it doesn't mean it has to be a report. It could be a checklist. It could be a video series. It could be a webinar, whatever your kind of front and lead magnet is, something that's going to let you know or let you know that that audience member is interested in the topic you can help them with. So then that your next step of offering them a chance to get on the phone with you or a member of your team makes sense for them. So here's some quick examples. And again, this lesson, we're not going to dive too deep into the images and copy and everything, but just some quick examples you see we have what we call our client acquisition report. That's when we use a case study. This is a webinar right here, another version of this client acquisition report. All of this is what we call our RTA funnel, our report to appointment. And we never want to lose sight of the fact of how well is this transitioning people into a phone call. Now, of the examples I'm going to go through, the different types of funnels today, the RTA funnel is the one that is going to let you grow your list the biggest. You'll get options at a cheaper cost for this than you will for the DTA, which we'll show here in just a second. But just keep that in mind. If you're looking to grow your list as you're pushing people towards appointments and those higher leverage opportunities, the RTA funnel is one that you're going to want to look at. Now the big thing that I mentioned on that funnel page was lead forms. You see here, there's an option like this down below. It'll say download. There's a number of other options we'll see. There's one that says get quotes, sign up, learn more, things like that. The option down here of this download button, that's set. I think there's four or five that LinkedIn will allow you. And you have to choose one of those. But again, the reason the lead forms are so successful is it lowers the barriers to entry. And when someone clicks that button, it shows up like this. Now the great thing about LinkedIn is it has really powerful data. You can target people based on their job title, the industry, the company size, all this great B2B information. Now it's also going to, when someone clicks that, it's going to fill in this information based on what they signed up for LinkedIn with. So you don't have to have them fill out their phone number, fill out their email address. This information is going to be populated automatically. You can add custom questions. You see here, there's an example. A question we'll use to qualify our leads is based on the current annual revenue they're doing. So you can add some custom questions there. But lead forms is the key to keeping your costs in line on LinkedIn.

## [06:00 - 09:02]

One of the keys that we're going over. Another example here of that, this one, it's a little tough to see on here, but get quotes is the call to action here. You see we have a little video that we run with this one. And again, we'll look more in depth at the types of copy and images that you're going to be using. So let's look at some benchmarks specifically for the RTA, the report to appointment funnel. As I mentioned, you might see some of these click numbers and be like, hey, I'm used to getting $2 clicks, $1 clicks on Facebook. As I said, the cost per clicks can be higher, but the reason you can live with that is that the quality of those appointments, the quality of the leads coming through, ideally is going to be stronger for you as well. So $4 to $8 a click is typically where we see those numbers lie. I will say $4 is a really strong, successful ad on LinkedIn. If you have people clicking for $4 to your report or to your webinar, I can almost guarantee you're going to be doing really well. From that, when we're offering at the next steps, someone to get on the phone or book an appointment, immediately or within five days is really where we set our metrics there. Typically, we're seeing $15 to $40 a lead. So someone that's filling out the opt-in, they're actually putting their information in and requesting this report. And then from there, the big one that we're always focused on and our clients are focused on is how much am I spending to get that person actually onto an appointment? So an RTA report to appointment funnel, it's going to have lower percentage of people that opt-in that end up booking the call, but at the same time, the good news is you're going to get more people that opt-in in the first place at a cheaper cost. So on the average, we see about $110 to $250 for someone that books an appointment. Those numbers can go up significantly if you start to add in some follow-up with phone calls as well. You saw on that lead-forming example, one of the questions we'll ask is their phone number, it'll be auto-populated by LinkedIn. If you call to follow-up with those people, it's something that we recommend every single one of our clients does or we help them set that up if that's the case, if they need it. All right, so let's take a look now at the DTA funnel. Again, that stands for direct to appointment. So this is one of the things that's great about this funnel is its simplicity. This we're just showing an ad to someone that is telling a little bit about what we do and giving the opportunity to sign up for a call right then and there. It's simple. At the same time, it's a bigger ask. So you're going to expect to spend a little bit more for each click and each opt-in. And as a result, this isn't the way that I would say is going to be the best way to grow your list. You're going to grow your list a bit, but if you're looking for lower opt-in numbers, this isn't the place I would say to start. So the DTA funnel, what does this look like? Again, you define your campaign avatar. I like to specifically have my niches for my different campaigns. You know, create a sponsored post and then finally you're going to complete the lead form. And from there, they're going to be sent in to pick their time on your calendar to talk with you or a member of your team. Here's an example of what this looks like with a video. You see our CEO and founder Josh Turner here. Very simple, very direct, very to the point. You see our headline here. Can we talk about getting you more leads?

## [09:02 - 12:05]

And a button there that says get quote. Again, the secret here, make sure you're using lead forms. This is a way that you can really keep your costs in line on LinkedIn and I highly recommend it to everyone. It's going to put in your information with the phone number and email for your leads. Again, if you want to add in a custom question, you can certainly do that. We'll use that to kind of make sure we're putting someone. We're pitching them the right type of product or service for them. And then from there, the next step is they're going to your calendar page. They're going to your page on your homepage that might be to sign up for their call and talk with your team. And then again, key for this one, you're going to see a higher percentage of people that actually booked the appointment because if they click the form to get a quote, most people are going to want to have that call, right? But still, not everyone will do that. So following up with your email follow-up campaigns, if you can do it with phone calls from your team, is really what's going to help you keep those costs in line and make you stay on the track to be an ROI positive. So let's look at some benchmarks here for the DTA funnel. You're going to see here, cost per clicks can be a little bit more high because again, it's a bigger ask, right? I'm going to ask this one right away to get on the phone, not just to download a report or visit a webinar. $7 to $12 a click is typically where we see that, $50 to $100 per lead. But because we see about usually half the people who opt in follow through and book their call within those five days, about $100 to $250 in appointment. So that's where things stand. Again, you're going to see the appointment numbers, typically the costs are in line. So it kind of comes down to what your focus is. If you're trying to grow your list, the RTA type might be a better one for you because you know you're going to get appointments at this cost and you're just looking to grow your list a lot as that goes. Let's look at the third route we're going to look at today is sponsored in mail. This is the best place where I can send people for a quick start. Typically what I'm going to look at today is about retargeting. So looking at people that have visited certain pages of your website and sending them a sponsored in mail. Now, you might be hearing that and thinking like, cool, I've heard a lot about in mail. That's part of LinkedIn's premium thing. I do want to just caution that this sponsored in mail is a lot different than just a regular in mail that someone with a sales navigator account or a premium account on regular LinkedIn will be. You're going to get to choose your audience just like any of the sponsored content ads and send it to large, large batches of people and there's some interesting formatting options they're going to give you that we'll look at as well. So the funnel here, person's going to visit a page on your site. Maybe they visited a contact us page. They visited your sales page, but they didn't take that next step. They didn't book a phone call with your team. They didn't check out and buy whatever it is you're offering, whatever it may be. And then you follow up with a sponsored in mail. The way those will work is the next time that person logs into their LinkedIn account, right there at the top of their inbox is going to be a message from you or a member of your sales team that kind of is going to point them towards getting back to this opportunity that they missed out on the last time. The first step, like anything as we said, you're going to choose your audience.

## [12:05 - 15:06]

What we're looking at here, your website audience, they also refer to them as matched audiences, similar to a custom audience in Facebook. It's just saying, you know, hey, people that have visited these certain URLs but didn't visit these other ones. So you'd say they didn't visit a thank you page on my contact us form or whatever it may be. So you go in, you choose the website audiences that you want for your campaign. And then the next step, as I said, would be putting in your message. And it looks like this. This is a sponsored in mail. You're going to see a number of things here that are a bit different. For starters, as I said, with a sponsored in mail versus your regular in mail, you get to choose your audience. So you can say, show this to, if it's a cold audience, you can say show this to all the CEOs of companies the size of 11 to 50 employees. If it's a retargeting type campaign, which is where I recommend people starting, is I want to show this ad to everyone that visited the contact us page but did not book a call. So that's the first thing that separates it from the regular in mail. The second is the clicks for this, for a sponsored in mail specifically with retargeting, you can get more for like a dollar or even 60 cents, which, again, if you're just starting, you're trying to get something and you're keeping a close eye on your budget, the cost per click here is going to be much more easy to swallow for many people than maybe the sponsored content ad. So this is a great place, I think, for people to start. And then the other thing that this sponsored in mail play works for, if you're going more to a colder audience, is it works for smaller audience pools. So if you're going after an audience pool that's only 500, 1,000, 1,500 types of people, or amount of people in there, this can be a really good route because, again, it gets a higher click-through rate, it's a higher open rate than some of the other routes, which usually want a slightly bigger audience. And again, we'll talk about audiences in a further lesson. As an example, this is what a regular in mail looks like. You see here, it just looks like any other message that's in my inbox. This will end up getting buried. And you have to go out there and find the individual people and send that by hand, write the McCusta message. It can take a lot of time as well. So again, the sponsored in mail, some big things to point out here. You get a call to action button. You can hyperlink text. Both of those, you can't do in a regular in mail. You can put a little banner ad here on the side. So a lot more items. And then again, the thing that I mentioned is this is going to stay at the top of their inbox when they log in. So it's not going to get buried if they haven't been under their link in for a little while. Again, another thing that that we find that is really, really good for the sponsored in mail. So let's look at some benchmarks specifically for the sponsored in mail funnel. And I mentioned this, you can get lower than that for sure. But $1.25 to $2 a click or open, as they refer to it there. And what we see from this retarding campaign, typically $30 to $65 an appointment. So a big cutoff, a big drop off there. Again, where I recommend, though, is mostly as a retargeting play for sponsored in mail, as a starting point and what we're going to look at over these lessons. And again, as you see there, retargeting.

## [15:06 - 17:03]

So let's take a look real quick at the other ad options that LinkedIn has available. A couple that I really want to point out. One dynamic ads. This is newer to LinkedIn. This is something you can have. You can put the name of the person that's looking at the ad. You can put their image like you see here. These are on the right sidebar. One of the things that's great about these dynamic ads, and I think is really interesting for the future of LinkedIn ads, is that that will stay pinned to the side of their sidebar as you're scrolling through the page. So it's a lot of nice real estate there. And it's one that we're starting to investigate. They just released that in the last couple months here. Here's another example of what that looks like. Again, you can put dynamic information and merge in their name or their images, or you can just have kind of more of a typical sidebar ad like this. Text Ad is the other one. Now, text ads look like this. It's either going to be on the sidebar. It's going to be at the top of their news feed. The click-through rate on these is really low. We don't spend a lot in this section. It's something that we have running at a low CPC bid, just because every now and then we'll get someone to click through there. It's part of what we refer to as Operation V Everywhere. Typically, we'll use these text ads as remark retargeting. So people that have been on our list for a while or they've checked out some of our pages but not taken the next step, we'll use this as another place of real estate to take advantage of on LinkedIn. So with all that in mind, I want you to remember this slide right here. With LinkedIn ads, to keep the costs in line, try and get people into a higher leverage opportunity as soon as possible, even if you're off the lead man in upfront. Make that thank you page. Make the follow-up be focused on getting them to either book an appointment or check out your sales opportunity, your sales page, your video sales layer, things like that. Appointments really are the golden ticket from what we've seen be successful on LinkedIn ads. So I hope that makes sense. And in the next lesson, we're going to walk through how to find your market, how to set up the audiences for what you sell and then the service that you provide.
